text: A stage name is typically bestowed on you soon after, if not right after, your master accepts you as a sign of you being formally under their care. It can, but doesn't have to, involve bits of your legal name (Yanagiya Kosan V famously gave most of his zenza the combination of 小 + an element of their legal name) Technically Akane isn't going by her legal name, as her stage name is in hiragana as contrasted to the kanji in her name. This sorta aligns with how many zenza tend to be given names at least partially in the (relatively) childish hiragana to emphasize their status as sort of children within their school, still having room for growth. And of course, there is the teigo (clan/school name) of Arakawa. (And while we're at it, PSA: do **NOT** refer to hanashika purely by their teigo.) I currently know of only one hanashika actually going by their legal given name and that's Hayashiya Sakutaro (and the fact he decided to do so was widely seen as quite unusual,) who is currently a futatsume.
label: analysis

text: **Rewatcher** Ah episode 5, the most LycoReco episode there is out there. What I mean by that, is that this is the first time, you have the entire café cast actually on screen and in action. We have Mika's leadership, Kurumi's tech support, Mizuki as intel and drive support, Chisato as the ace in combat and Takina as her capable mission focused partner. The 5 are quite the well oiled machine. Now for the plot, it is quite heavy, with Shinji making his play. And his plan is not bad, just because he does not know Chisato as well as other, he misunderstands how much she has grown, and set she is in being herself. It won't be so easy to make her kill. The world building and politics also start to heat up. Political apathy is the name of the game, and again, while tempting, this solely relies on the ones in charge actually prioritizing stability and good governance over power struggles. And with the ending of Majima applying pressure to DA, maybe they will crack and become draconian thus justifying him. We'll see. Now for the dialogue, Takina's concern looks so genuine when Chisato did not tell her about her heart. Takina is quite attentive, in every word of that definition if we consider the ending. I love how she tries to cheer Chisato up at the end. Takina is quite perspective to emotions, it may because of episode 3, where she suffered a break down, it could her emulating Chisato, or she could have actually been quite the kind person inherently. Still Chisato brought that to t

text: **Second time at the cafe** As usual in this episode we get a very clever blend of SoL and action by having our ‘mission’ be a tour around Tokyo. We get a decent amount of time to roam around and let Chisataki have fun, before inevitably something goes wrong and we have some more stellar gunfight action between Jin and our team. Once again I commend the pacing, clever use of our entire cast of characters (shoutout to Mizuki for trying her best), decent twists and suspenseful moments to keep you guessing throughout the entire sequence. I actually forgot that the reveal of Chisato’s artificial heart was done so early, so I guess even as a rewatcher I was taken by surprise at Chisato randomly dropping it. I imagine the response of most first timers will mirror Takina’s “wait what?” The question gets partially answered throughout the episode with Mika explaining it to Kurumi and Chisato/Takina’s conversation on the boat. However, we are now left with further questions about its link to the Allen institute and Chisato’s ‘mission’. Very effective setup and payoffs that make me remember how I managed to binge this series so fast during my first watch. There is a bit more exploration of how the societal hierarchy works - Abe as a police detective is aware of the covering up happening, but chooses to be complacent and not prod into it, thinking that the ‘ends justifies the means’. Meanwhile this explanation takes the other detective by surprise and his first reaction is horror and bet

text: The Wiseman's grandchild should be the baseline for generic isekai. It explains multiple reasons why MC is overpowered like that hes raised by the 2 most powerful magic users and how his magic is boosted by his scientific understanding of the spells and how they affect the world. There is no harem at all, the guy gets one girl interested in him and a bunch of classmates of both genders that he teaches to also be overpowered. And the fan service is minimal. During the obligatory hotspring episode, the character that gets a pan of their naked body (obscured by water) is the grandma. The show is by no means amazing, but it does it's job well and doesn't really make any mistakes or offend anyone. Its a solid baseline for a 6/10. Better than average, but not by much
label: analysis

text: They were only able to massacre the village in that fashion because Asa broke the seal with break; Asa is just as complicit in the murders of everyone in the village as Gabby is. Why does she get a pass? I think the narrative treating Gabby (and most of the Kagemori's) with agency outside of just being one-dimensional killers is a good thing contrary to the this kind of thinking I often see. The narrative is treating the Kagemori's in a neutral light instead of guiding the viewer on how to feel about them. I feel like this is just "trust in the viewer" and not anything sinister like excusing their actions and people aren't used to this level of neutrality with these topics. The series does not whitewash the fact that they're lowbrow Yakuza, yet the fact remains that they all think they're right/justified in their own way so of course we see scenes of them chilling, hanging out, and acting in a sympathetic light. To them, they're the protagonists of their story. They're justified and their crimes don't weigh on them, that is how they (and actual "murderers with a code") continue to smile day to day in real life. Despite their crimes they're all humans with their own justification and not caricatures of whatever you think murderers should be. You're supposed to come to your own feelings on them by judging their actions day to day instead of the author automatically condemning them and going "these guys are all evil". Am I the only one who finds it to be mature writing? We don't
label: analysis

text: It’s really hard to take a lot of what’s happening seriously. I mean. We’ve got people trying to assault a compound full of heavily armed insurgents with knives and swords. Like atleast give a lore reason why they’re using swords. Like the seasons give them a barrier or magic or something. Otherwise it looks goofy seeing plain clothed people trying to fight insurgents. Like can the seasons people not afford a couple hundred for a plate carrier and plates for it? The average American is better equipped than any of these people. Just insane. Also why are they assaulting this place on their own without any sort of air support or other support? Hey have aircraft hence “air ambulance” like what the hell. The one thing that did surprise me that I’m happy happened was bringing the summer girl back to life. This show has had so much overly forced torture, trauma, and sad stuff that her death didn’t even feel that sad cause honestly I’m just expecting it at this point cause the author has over done it with trying to force things.
